title: NOC 13200 – Customs, Ship, and Other Brokers | GTR Immigration
description: Customs Brokers, Shipbrokers, and related professionals play a crucial role in Canada’s global trade and logistics network. Classified under NOC 13200, this occupation continues to see steady growth following the post-pandemic recovery and offers viable immigration pathways toward Permanent Residence (PR) through Canada’s skilled worker programs and Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs).

classification.teer: 3 – Occupations usually require a college diploma or apprenticeship training of less than two years; or more than six months of on-the-job training
classification.major_group: 132 – Transportation and production logistics coordinators and customs and related broker occupations
classification.minor_group: 1320 – Transportation and production logistics coordinators and customs and related broker occupations

common_job_titles: Cargo broker Chartered shipbroker Customs broker Gas broker Licensed customs broker Shipbroker Ship line agent
duties: Prepare and submit import/export documentation in line with customs regulations
Sign customs forms using power of attorney
Arrange payment of duties, taxes, bonds, storage, and transportation
Quote tariff rates and customs charges
Advise clients on import/export restrictions, insurance, and trade rules
Represent clients before government agencies or tribunals
Buy and sell cargo space on vessels
Arrange ship charters and cargo movement
Buy and sell vessels or yachts
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school and some post-secondary education in commerce or a related field
Customs broker licensing from the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)
Completion of customs broker training programs
Shipbrokers generally require extensive industry experience
Courses from the Institute of Chartered Shipbrokers may be beneficial
job_outlook: Between 2022 and 2031, Canada is projected to generate 20,200 new positions for Customs, Ship, and Other Brokers, compared with 17,900 job seekers—making strategic province selection and immigration planning especially important for overseas applicants.
additional_information: Customs, Ship, and Other Brokers are employed by customs brokerage firms, shipping agencies, logistics companies, and may also be self-employed.

Continued hiring in international trade and logistics sectorsStrong demand for customs-licensed professionalsEligibility for Express Entry and Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs)Employer-supported work permit optionsAnnual earnings commonly ranging from $30,800 to $61,800 CAD
Why Choose Canada in This Occupation?

Universal public healthcareSecure employment in trade-focused industriesCompetitive compensationStrong labour protectionsBalanced work schedulesMulticultural port and logistics hubsClear pathways from work permits to Permanent Residence
